title: Writing Clear Requirements
intro: You need to describe your task manager to an AI assistant. It's like giving directions to a taxi driver — say 'somewhere nice' and you might end up in a parking lot, but give exact GPS coordinates and you arrive exactly where you want. The quality of your description determines the quality of what gets built. Make good choices.

teach.title: Requirements = Your App's Blueprint
teach.body: Requirements are a clear list of what your app should do. Without them, you'll wander, forget features, and build the wrong thing. When using AI to help you code, requirements become even more critical — the AI builds exactly what you describe, nothing more.

teach.title: GPS Coordinates vs. 'Go That Way'
teach.body: Giving vague requirements to AI is like telling a taxi driver 'take me somewhere nice.' You might end up at a fine restaurant or a random parking lot. Clear requirements are like giving exact GPS coordinates — the driver knows precisely where to go, and you arrive exactly where you want to be.

teach.title: Bad vs Good Requirements
teach.code:
BAD:  "Make a task app"
GOOD: "Users can create tasks with a title (required)
      and description (optional). Tasks have three
      states: todo, in-progress, done. Users can
      filter tasks by state."
teach.annotation: The bad requirement gives the AI nothing to work with. The good one specifies exactly what 'task' means and how it behaves.

teach.body: Clear requirements are your superpower as a vibecoder. While others waste hours going back and forth with AI, you'll nail it in one prompt and ship your product faster — time saved is money earned.
Good requirements answer: What can the user DO? What DATA does each thing have? What are the STATES or RULES? Write these down before you prompt any AI.

start.narrative: You're ready to describe your task manager to an AI coding assistant. You open the chat and think about how to start. What do you write first?

choices.text: "I need a task manager with these features: ..." and list the core features
"Use React and PostgreSQL to create a CRUD app for tasks"

vague-result.narrative: The AI generates a very basic todo app with just a text input and a list. No priorities, no due dates, no status tracking. It works, but it's not what you wanted. What do you do?

choices.text: Start over with a detailed prompt describing exactly what you need
Ask the AI to 'make it better' without specifics

features-result.narrative: Good start! The AI asks a follow-up question: 'Should tasks have due dates? How should they be sorted?' How do you respond?

choices.text: "Yes, tasks have: title (required), description (optional), due date (optional), priority (low/medium/high), status (todo/in-progress/done). Default sort by priority then due date."

technical-result.narrative: The AI starts building, but you only told it the tech stack, not what the app should actually do. It creates a generic CRUD interface. The data model has only 'title' and 'completed'. What now?

choices.text: Add the missing fields and behaviors one by one in follow-up prompts
Write a complete feature spec first, then give it to the AI in one clear prompt

edge-cases.narrative: Your spec is taking shape, but the AI flags questions about edge cases — what shows when the task list is empty? What if a title is 500 characters? What if a task has no due date? How do you handle this?

choices.text: Add an 'Edge Cases' section: empty list shows an onboarding prompt, titles capped at 100 chars, due dates default to none, max 100 tasks per list
Tell the AI to 'use sensible defaults for edge cases'
Skip edge cases — fix them when bugs come up

validation.narrative: Before the AI writes a single line of code, you want to confirm it understood your requirements. What's your move?

choices.text: Ask: 'Restate the data model and feature list in your own words. Don't write code yet.'
Trust the AI got it and ask it to start coding
Ask: 'Are my requirements clear?' — a yes/no question the AI will always answer 'yes' to

iteration.narrative: Halfway through building, you realize you forgot a requirement: tasks need to be assignable to other users. How do you communicate this change?

choices.text: Write a clear delta: 'New requirement: tasks have an assignee field (optional, references user.id). Update the data model, the create/edit forms, and add a filter by assignee.'
Just say: 'also tasks should have an assignee'
Scrap the project and start over with a fresh prompt that includes everything

excellent-end.narrative: Your requirements are now a living spec — clear features, edge cases addressed, validated by the AI before any code, and updated with structured deltas as you discover new needs. The AI builds exactly what you intended, and future changes are easy to communicate.
excellent-end.feedback: This is professional-level requirements work. The decisions you made — specify features upfront, address edge cases explicitly, validate the AI's understanding before coding, and communicate changes as structured deltas — are how engineers ship products on the first try.

mediocre-end.narrative: The AI adds an assignee field, but interprets it loosely — it ends up as a free-text string instead of a reference to a user. You'll need to refactor it later. The product works, but the data model is inconsistent.
mediocre-end.feedback: Quick deltas feel fast in the moment, but they leave the AI guessing. A short structured update — 'field name, type, what it references, where it appears' — takes 30 seconds and prevents an hour of refactoring later.

wasteful-end.narrative: You scrap the project and rewrite the prompt from scratch. The AI builds it again — but now you've thrown away every small fix you made manually. You lost half a day of progress over one missing field.
wasteful-end.feedback: Restarting from scratch is rarely necessary. Requirements are meant to evolve. Learn to write structured updates that the AI can apply incrementally, and your project will keep growing instead of resetting.
